ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE. PROJECT REPORT 20, APRIL 2026 1
Face Recognition for Login Authentication
Vineela Gujjarlapudi, AP23110011644, Jai Manish Kumar Bikkina , AP2311001073, Gangadhar Bandaru,
AP23110010344,
Abstract—This project presents a comprehensive face
recognition-based authentication system that integrates biometric
and traditional password-based security mechanisms for en-
hanced user verification. The objective is to develop a secure,
user-friendly web application that combines facial recognition
technology with liveness detection to prevent spoofing attacks and
unauthorized access. The system employs OpenCV for image pro-
cessing, dlib’s 128-dimensional ResNet-based face descriptor for
face encoding, and Flask as the backend framework, with SQLite
for credential storage. Duri

et-based face descriptor for
face encoding, and Flask as the backend framework, with SQLite
for credential storage. During registration, users capture a live
facial image via browser camera; the system extracts a unique
face encoding (128-d vector) and stores it alongside a hashed
password. For authentication, users submit both credentials and
a live face capture while responding to randomly assigned liveness
challenges (blink or nod). The system validates the liveness
response through eye aspect ratio and head position metrics
extracted from sequential video frames, achieving robust anti-
spoofing detection. Upon successful liveness verification, the login
face is compared against the stored encoding using face distance
measurements (threshold: 0.5), and access is granted only if
both pas

ed against the stored encoding using face distance
measurements (threshold: 0.5), and access is granted only if
both password and face distance criteria are satisfied. Testing
with two registered users confirmed accurate face matching,
secure password hashing using scrypt, and effective liveness
challenge enforcement. The dashboard enables users to update
their password and face profile post-authentication. Results
demonstrate that the integrated biometric-password approach
significantly reduces unauthorized access risk while maintaining
user convenience.This project provides a practical foundation for
multi-factor biometric authentication systems and highlights the
feasibility of deploying facial recognition in web applications for
enhanced cybersecurity.
Index Terms—Face recognition, Bio

bility of deploying facial recognition in web applications for
enhanced cybersecurity.
Index Terms—Face recognition, Biometric authentication,
Liveness detection, Face encoding, OpenCV , dlib, Password
hashing, Anti-spoofing, Web security, Flask, SQLite, Eye aspect
ratio, Face distance metric
I. INTRODUCTION
W
ITH the exponential growth of digital transactions
and the increasing sophistication of cyber threats,
traditional password-based authentication has become
insufficient for secure access control. Single-factor
authentication (username and password) falls victim to
social engineering, phishing attacks, password reuse, and
brute-force attempts. Conversely, multi-factor authentication
(MFA) combining something the user knows (password)
with something the user is (biometrics) provides su

r authentication
(MFA) combining something the user knows (password)
with something the user is (biometrics) provides substantially
stronger security postures. Among biometric modalities, face
recognition has emerged as a user-friendly, non-intrusive
technology increasingly adopted in mobile devices, border
security, and enterprise access systems. However, face
recognition systems remain vulnerable to spoofing attacks
using printed photographs, video replays, and deepfakes.
This necessitates the integration of liveness detection
mechanisms that verify the presence of a living person
during authentication. The objective of this work is to
develop and validate a comprehensive face recognition-based
authentication system that combines password verification,
facial encoding matching, and liven

ve face recognition-based
authentication system that combines password verification,
facial encoding matching, and liveness detection to create
a robust, practical authentication framework suitable for
web-based applications.
The technological foundation of this project integrates
several well-established libraries and frameworks. OpenCV
4.10.0.84 serves as the primary image processing engine for
capturing, decoding, and manipulating video frames from
browser-based camera streams. The face recognition library
(version 1.3.0), built upon dlib, provides state-of-the-art face
detection using Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) and
face encoding using a ResNet-based 128-dimensional facial
descriptor. NumPy 2.1.2 handles numerical computations
for face distance calculations and liveness metri

ensional facial
descriptor. NumPy 2.1.2 handles numerical computations
for face distance calculations and liveness metric extraction.
Flask 3.1.0 provides the web framework backend, while
SQLite manages persistent storage of user credentials
and face encodings. For password security, scrypt-based
hashing (via werkzeug.security) replaces plaintext storage.
Alternative approaches considered but not implemented
include: (1) CNN-based face detection (more accurate but
computationally expensive for browser environments), (2) iris
recognition (requires specialized hardware), (3) fingerprint
matching (incompatible with web-only deployment), and (4)
behavioral biometrics (less reliable than geometric biometrics).
The experimental methodology involved registering two
user subjects (Vineela and sria

liable than geometric biometrics).
The experimental methodology involved registering two
user subjects (Vineela and sriakshaya) through the system’s
registration interface. During registration, each subject
submitted a live facial capture via browser camera; the
system extracted the 128-dimensional face encoding and
paired it with a scrypt-hashed password for storage in
the SQLite database. Login authentication required users
to provide both password and live camera capture while
responding to randomly assigned liveness challenges: either
a natural blink or a head nod motion. The system captured
six sequential frames at 220 millisecond intervals, extracting
facial landmarks, eye aspect ratios (EAR), and head position
shifts (nod metric). Face distance was computed using
Euclidean distance

ks, eye aspect ratios (EAR), and head position
shifts (nod metric). Face distance was computed using
Euclidean distance between the login encoding and stored
encoding (threshold: 0.5). Liveness validation thresholds
were empirically determined: for blink challenges, minimum
EAR drop of 0.05 with minimum eye closure of 0.20; for
nod challenges, minimum head position span of 0.11. Final
authentication required simultaneous satisfaction of password
match, liveness verification, and face distance criteria.
Results demonstrated successful authentication for both

ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE. PROJECT REPORT 20, APRIL 2026 2
registered users across multiple login attempts, with the
system correctly rejecting invalid credentials and detecting
presentation attacks. The integrated approach combining three
independent verification factors—password, biometric match-
ing, and liveness detection—provides a practical, deployable
authentication system suitable for web applications requiring
heightened security. This work validates the feasibility of
browser-based facial recognition combined with anti-spoofing
measures, establishing a foundation for future multi-modal
biometric systems in web environments.
II. MATERIALS ANDMETHODS
This section describes the experimental setup, system in-
tegration, data acquisition process, and calculations used to
implement and e

scribes the experimental setup, system in-
tegration, data acquisition process, and calculations used to
implement and evaluate the face-recognition-based authenti-
cation workflow. The objective was to build a secure login
pipeline that combines password verification, facial matching,
and liveness validation to reduce spoofing riskKulkarni2015.
A. Materials
The experiment used a standard laptop/desktop with
an RGB camera for image capture. Software compo-
nents included Python 3.11, Flask 3.1.0 (backend service),
OpenCV 4.10.0.84 (image decoding and preprocessing),
face-recognition 1.3.0 with dlib (face landmarks and 128-
dimensional embeddings), NumPy 2.1.2 (numerical opera-
tions), and SQLite (local persistence for user records). The
web interface was implemented with HTML, CSS, and
Jav

pera-
tions), and SQLite (local persistence for user records). The
web interface was implemented with HTML, CSS, and
JavaScript for browser-side camera control and frame submis-
sion.
B. System Connection and Procedure
The system was connected in the following sequence:
camera stream→browser capture module→Flask server
→liveness and face-processing module→SQLite lookup→
authentication decision.
Registration was performed by capturing one live facial
frame, extracting a single face embedding, hashing the user
password with scrypt, and storing the resulting data in the
database. During login, the user provided username and pass-
word, while the client captured a short frame sequence for
liveness and one frame for final face matching. A random
challenge (blink or nod) was assigned per session

sequence for
liveness and one frame for final face matching. A random
challenge (blink or nod) was assigned per session. Authenti-
cation was granted only when all checks passed.
C. Measurements
The following measurements were taken during each login
attempt:
Eye geometry over sequential frames (for blink detection).
Nose-to-eye normalized vertical motion (for nod detection).
Euclidean distance between login embedding and stored em-
bedding. Final Boolean authentication outcome (accept/reject)
from rule-based fusion.

as:

Euclidean norm.

Face matching used embedding distance:
D=∥elogin−estored∥ 2 (3)
where e login and e stored are 128-D face embeddings.
Final access decision was:
A=P∧L∧(D≤τ)(4)
where P is password-valid flag, L is liveness-valid flag, and T
=0.5 is the face-match threshold. For this session, blink and
nod thresholds were applied to the frame sequence, and only
users satisfying all constraints were authenticated.
E. Result of Method Execution
Using this method, registered users were authenticated
successfully when password, liveness response, and facial
similarity were all

ethod, registered users were authenticated
successfully when password, liveness response, and facial
similarity were all valid. Attempts with invalid credentials,
insufficient liveness evidence, or non-matching facial embed-
dings were rejected. This confirms that the combined multi-
factor biometric pipeline is operational and suitable for secure
web-login experimentation.

Figure1 shows face distance versus attempt order (time
sequence). In Fig.1, accepted points remain mostly below the
threshold line, while rejected points appear when the distance
exceeds T=0.50. This indicates that the chosen threshold
separates matching and non-matching attempts effectively for
this session.

Fig. 1: Face distance D D versus attempt index with threshold
= 0.50 =0.50.
Liveness was still a required verification factor in this
experiment. For each login, the user completed a challenge
(blink or nod), and the backend accepted access only when
password verification, liveness validation, and face match were
all satisfied. Therefore, even if a distance value was below
threshold, access could still be rejected when liveness failed.
Figure 2 presents the developed web interface used during
the experiment (camera-based login page and workflow view),
showing the practical implementation of the authentication
pipeline.

Overall, the combined method (password + liveness + face
matching) improves login reliability and reduces spoofing risk.
The strongest technical outcome is that access is granted only
when all verification conditions are simultaneously satisfied.
IV. DISCUSSION ANDSUMMARY
The primary objective of this experiment was to design
and validate a secure web-based authentication system that
combines password verification, facial biometric matching,
and liveness detection. This objective was achieved to a high
degree for the tested operating conditions. The implemented
pipeline consistently accepted valid users when all three
criteria were satisfied and rejected invalid or non-compliant
attempts when e

tently accepted valid users when all three
criteria were satisfied and rejected invalid or non-compliant
attempts when either password, liveness, or facial similarity
failed. This confirms that multi-factor fusion is more robust
than single-factor authentication.
An important result is the clear decision separation pro-
duced by the face-distance threshold. In engineering terms,
the system exhibited goodclassification accuracyon the
session test attempts, with stable pass/fail behavior around the
selected threshold T = 0.50. Theprecisionof decision-making
improved because a biometric match alone was not sufficient;
the liveness gate reduced spoof susceptibility. The temporal
liveness signals (blink EAR variation and nod displacement
span) also demonstrated usefulmeasurement resolution, sin

ral
liveness signals (blink EAR variation and nod displacement
span) also demonstrated usefulmeasurement resolution, since
small frame-to-frame changes were captured and used in rule-
based classification.
From a measurement perspective, reliability depends on
acquisition quality (camera framing, illumination, motion blur,
and face visibility). These factors influence embedding con-
sistency and landmark stability, and therefore affect bothre-
peatabilityandfalse decision rates. Even so, the present setup
produced operationally consistent outcomes for enrolled users
in normal indoor conditions. Password handling via scrypt
hashing further strengthened security by protecting credential
confidentiality in storage.
The experiment validates key principles from biometric
security theory: (1) te

ng credential
confidentiality in storage.
The experiment validates key principles from biometric
security theory: (1) template-space distance can be used as
a practical similarity metric for identity verification, (2) tem-
poral physiological motion improves resistance to presentation
attacks, and (3) logical AND fusion of heterogeneous factors
improves overall trust in authentication outcomes.
Applications include secure login for academic portals,
internal enterprise dashboards, attendance and access control
systems, and lightweight identity verification for web services.
Recommended future work includes larger-population testing,
ROC/EER-based threshold tuning, adaptive per-user thresh-
olds, anti-deepfake challenge expansion, encrypted template
storage, and optional CNN/GPU acceleratio

e per-user thresh-
olds, anti-deepfake challenge expansion, encrypted template
storage, and optional CNN/GPU acceleration for improved
robustness under varied pose and lighting conditions.
